text: Welcome to this video of a robotic right upper lobe lobectomy with chest wall resection, performed by Vijay Joshi and Joel Dunning and edited by Jason Trevis. We present the case of a seventy five year old female with a highly PET positive peripherally located tumour. As a result, the plan was to perform a wed resection of the tumour and send for frozen section, with scope to perform a full chest wall resection if there were inadequate margins.
The surgeon uses a stapler to perform the wedge resection of the tumor, with the final staple firing completing the resection.
The surgeon begins the resection of the chest wall, using a spatula in arm one and cadia forceps in arm two to dissect around the second and third ribs, aiming for clear margins.

Following the completion of the wedge resection, the surgeon proceeds with the right upper lobe lobectomy. The right upper lobe vein is transected using a Covidian Tan stapler, followed by the truncal branch of the pulmonary artery, which is also stapled using a Tan-45 Covidian stapler.
The surgeon dissects the subcarinal station seven lymph node, followed by the removal of the station R-ten lymph node located anterior to the pulmonary artery and inferior to the azygos vein.
A sling is placed around the right upper lobe bronchus, and a curved tip stapler is used for the final firing to secure the bronchus.

The posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery is visible. The surgeon completes the arterial and bronchial firings and proceeds to the fissure between the upper and middle lobe, performing staple firings from posterior to anterior.
After the final firing, the surgeon places the resected lobe into a surgical bag for removal from the thoracic cavity.
The surgical team receives the frozen section results, indicating a 0.1 millimeter margin, which is deemed inadequate. Consequently, they proceed with the chest wall resection.

The surgeon exposes the second and third ribs posteriorly, using a Gigli wire to pass behind each rib. The intercostal arteries are identified and cauterized. An additional incision is made beneath the axilla, and robotic arms are used to stabilize the Gigli saw during its gentle application.
The surgeon uses a jiggly saw to cut through the third rib, followed by cutting the second rib posteriorly. The robotic arms assist in stabilizing the saw during the procedure.

The surgeon uses the axillary incision to cut the ribs anteriorly with a rib cutter. The ribs are then dissected off the muscles of the anterior chest wall, with assistance pushing the ribs to ensure cutting in the correct plane.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the second and third ribs, positioned under the scapula, without requiring any patching.
Final histology revealed that there was no residual tumour remaining in the chest wall, ensuring that we'd achieved adequate margins in this T-III N-naught M-naught tumour resection.
The external view shows the placement of standard robotic ports along with an additional auxiliary incision beneath the axilla.
